messages.content: My board has 41 cards from Run job search and every one is a C or D fit. Settings: target titles 'revenue manager, pricing manager', keywords 'pricing, forecasting, revenue', seniority 'Manager', locations 'Denver, Remote'. I do hotel revenue management. The scan keeps returning SaaS pricing and airline yield roles. I burned all 10 searches yesterday on the same keyword set.

Bare 'pricing' is pulling SaaS and airline. Split tomorrow's 10 runs into three narrower passes instead of ten identical ones. In Settings, replace keywords with 'RevPAR, length of stay, rate parity, OTA channel' and delete 'pricing, forecasting'. Add 'director of revenue strategy' and 'revenue optimization' to target titles, and raise seniority to Manager plus Director so you see the step up. Then work two channels the scan does not cover: go direct to the career pages of resort and hotel management groups, and search LinkedIn for "revenue management" AND hotel -software. Log those with Add role so the board shows every channel.

Nothing is moving. 28 cards sat in Sourced for three weeks, 4 in Applied with no reply. I run clinical operations for infusion centers in Columbus. Settings titles are 'operations manager, clinical manager', keywords 'healthcare operations, patient throughput', locations 'Columbus'. I keep running the search daily and adding more Sourced cards.

The problem is not sourcing, it is that you keep sourcing. Stop at two runs a day and spend the rest of the time moving cards right. Pick the six strongest and research each one properly before applying. Widen locations to 'Columbus, Cincinnati, Dayton, Remote' so the funnel is not one metro deep. Add 'director of ambulatory operations' and 'clinical operations manager' as titles, and add 'Epic, chair time, survey readiness' to keywords so the scan reads for substance. For the four in Applied, find the hiring manager on LinkedIn and send a short note. Sourced cards that nobody has touched in two weeks should be Closed.

Too few results. Run job search returns 2 or 3 roles a day and they are all already on my board. I am a staff backend engineer on payments ledgers in Austin. Settings: titles 'staff software engineer, principal engineer', keywords 'Go, Kafka, double-entry ledger, PCI-DSS', seniority 'Principal', locations 'Austin'.

You have over-filtered. Four narrow keywords plus one city plus one seniority band leaves almost nothing to match. Drop 'double-entry ledger' and 'PCI-DSS' from keywords, keep 'Go, Kafka', and add 'payments, distributed systems'. Set seniority to Principal and Senior so staff-level postings stop being excluded. Add 'Remote' to locations, which is where most payments infrastructure roles sit. Use two of your ten daily runs on the broader set and see what volume looks like before narrowing again. Separately, go straight to the careers pages of payments and banking infrastructure companies, since those teams often post roles that never reach an aggregator.

Wrong locations. I run a 420-person plant in Grand Rapids and every result is Remote or out of state. Settings locations say 'Remote, Michigan'. Titles are 'plant manager, operations director'. I cannot relocate and plant jobs are not remote.

Remote in your locations field is doing all the damage, because it is the one term that matches everything and manufacturing leadership is on-site by definition. Delete Remote. Replace 'Michigan' with the specific commutable markets: 'Grand Rapids, Holland, Kalamazoo, Lansing'. Add 'VP of manufacturing operations' to titles so you catch the step up, and add 'OEE, IATF 16949, injection molding' to keywords so the scan reads for your actual environment. Two or three runs a day is enough at this narrowness. For coverage the scan misses, check the careers pages of tier-one automotive and appliance suppliers in those four markets directly and add anything you find with Add role.

High ghost risk everywhere. Most of my board is public agency postings that have been open four or five months, and two I applied to in March are still listed. I manage transit capital delivery in Portland. Titles 'program manager, capital projects director', locations 'Portland, Oregon'.

Long-open public postings are usually real but slow, not fake, so treat them as low-priority rather than dead. Before applying, check the agency's own careers page for the posting date and whether the recruitment is continuous. Anything continuous goes to the back of the queue. Reserve your daily runs for two or three passes and spend the saved time on the channel that actually works in public sector: watch the board and commission agendas of the agencies you want, since new positions get authorized there weeks before they post. Add 'capital programs director' and 'infrastructure delivery' as titles, and add neighboring jurisdictions to locations.

Everything the search returns wants 3 to 5 years as an analyst. I am a store operations lead moving into ops analytics, with SQL and Tableau I taught myself. Settings titles: 'data analyst, business analyst'. Keywords 'SQL, Tableau, analytics'. Locations 'Charlotte'. All C fits.

Generic analyst titles put you against people who have done the job for years. Aim at roles where your store background is the advantage. Change titles to 'retail operations analyst, operations analyst, business analyst distribution' and add 'retail operations, labor model, shrink, inventory accuracy' to keywords so the scan surfaces postings that want floor experience. Keep SQL and Tableau but let them be the second filter, not the first. Add 'Remote' plus 'Charlotte, Raleigh' to locations. Use three runs a day on that set. Outside the app, search LinkedIn for "operations analyst" retail Charlotte and go straight to the careers pages of regional retail and distribution employers.

My board is 60 cards and nothing is above a C. I am a VP of supply chain in Memphis running six DCs. Settings titles: 'supply chain manager, logistics manager, operations manager'. Seniority 'Manager'. Keywords 'supply chain, logistics, warehouse'.

Your seniority filter is set three levels below you, so the scan is doing exactly what you asked and returning manager roles. Set seniority to VP and Director, and change titles to 'vice president supply chain, SVP supply chain, chief supply chain officer'. Replace the generic keywords with 'network design, S&OP, distribution network, cost per case'. That will cut volume hard, which is correct at this level, so two runs a day is plenty. Executive roles also surface late on public boards. Ask two former colleagues to flag searches in progress, and check the careers pages of manufacturers and 3PLs in your region directly.

Too few results and the ones I get are junior. Senior product manager on a B2B operations platform in Seattle. Titles 'product manager'. Keywords 'product management, roadmap, SaaS'. Seniority '—'. Locations 'Seattle'.

An empty seniority setting plus the bare title 'product manager' means the scan cannot tell a first PM role from a director role, so it returns the most common thing, which is junior. Set seniority to Senior and Director. Change titles to 'senior product manager, principal product manager, director of product'. Swap keywords to 'B2B, workflow automation, net revenue retention, API product' so the match reads your actual area. Add 'Remote' to locations, since a large share of senior product roles are remote-first. Three runs a day on that set. Then check the careers pages of mid-market B2B software companies directly, and add what you find with Add role.

Wrong locations. I am director of nursing for post-acute in Baltimore. Settings locations 'Remote' because I wanted flexibility. Everything returned is remote utilization review and telehealth, none of it operational nursing leadership.

Running a facility is on-site work, so 'Remote' is matching utilization review and telehealth — a different profession, not a different location. Set locations to 'Baltimore, Columbia, Towson, Annapolis', plus southern Pennsylvania if you would drive it. Titles should read 'director of nursing, VP clinical operations, chief nursing officer'. Add 'skilled nursing, CMS five-star, MDS, survey readiness' to keywords so the scan separates post-acute from general clinical work. Once the field is that narrow, two of your ten daily runs will find everything there is. Seats like these usually move through nursing leadership networks before a posting exists, so work those conversations in parallel and log anything real with Add role.

I get maybe one new card a week. Airline network planning analyst in Chicago. Titles 'network planning analyst'. Keywords 'network planning, schedule, slot filing, Sabre'. Locations 'Chicago'. The 10 daily searches feel wasted.

One exact title plus four specialist keywords plus one city is close to a zero-match filter. Network planning is a small field, so widen the shape of the role rather than the geography. Titles should read 'network planning, schedule planning, capacity planning, revenue management analyst'. Cut 'Sabre' and 'slot filing' from keywords and keep 'network planning, route profitability'. Add 'Dallas, Atlanta, Remote' to locations, since carriers concentrate in a few headquarters cities. Two runs a day covers it. The bigger lever is direct. Carriers, rail and coach operators advertise on their own sites well ahead of any aggregator, so check those weekly and use Add role.

All C and D fits. Compliance manager in consumer lending, Salt Lake City. Titles 'compliance manager, compliance officer'. Keywords 'compliance, regulatory, risk'. Seniority 'Manager'. I get bank branch compliance and healthcare compliance roles.

Those three keywords exist in every industry, which is why you are getting hospital and branch roles. Replace them with 'TILA, UDAAP, fair lending, NMLS, consumer lending' so the scan reads your actual regulatory perimeter. Keep the titles but add 'director of regulatory affairs' so you see the level above. Set seniority to Manager and Director. Add 'Remote' to locations, since a lot of lending compliance is remote or hybrid. Three runs a day. For the roles that never hit a board, check the careers pages of nonbank lenders and fintechs directly, and ask your compliance network which teams are building out testing functions right now.

Nothing is moving and I have 22 cards in Sourced. Quality engineer, machining and aerospace fasteners, Greenville SC. I keep running the search because it feels productive but I have applied to two roles in six weeks.

You are using the search as a substitute for applying. Cap yourself at two runs a day and make the rule that no new card gets added until three existing ones have moved. Sort your 22 by whether you meet the stated experience band, pick the top five, and get a match report on each before writing anything. Anything you would not apply to this month should go to Closed so the board stops looking full. Quality engineering roles also move through supplier networks: ask the quality managers at two of your customers whether they are hiring, and add those with Add role.

High ghost risk. Half my board is customer success leadership roles at companies that reposted the same job three times this year. Head of customer success in Boston. I do not want to waste applications.

Repeated reposts usually mean the role is real but the bar or the comp is wrong, not that the job is fake. Before applying, check whether the company's own careers page still shows it and whether the team page shows someone already in the seat. If both look stale, deprioritize. Spend two runs a day rather than ten and put the time into the channel that works at this level: senior customer success roles are filled through networks, so tell four people in your industry you are looking. Add 'VP customer success, head of retention' to titles and 'gross retention, Gainsight, expansion' to keywords.
Wrong locations. Transportation manager in Fort Worth. Settings locations 'Texas'. I am getting Houston, El Paso and Amarillo roles I cannot take, and missing things 30 minutes away.

A whole state is too coarse a filter, and it is both letting the wrong things in and hiding the right ones. Replace 'Texas' with the specific commutable set: 'Fort Worth, Dallas, Arlington, Grand Prairie, Irving'. Add 'Remote' only if you would genuinely take a network role that travels. While you are in Settings, add 'director of transportation' to titles so you see the step up, and add 'TMS, carrier procurement, on-time in full, cost per mile' to keywords. Three runs a day is enough at that geography. Distributors and 3PLs across those five markets tend to advertise in-house ahead of the boards, so check their sites directly.

Too few results. Grants and contracts administrator, Albuquerque. Titles 'grants administrator'. Keywords 'Uniform Guidance, SF-425, subrecipient monitoring, single audit'. Locations 'Albuquerque'. Some days the search returns nothing new.

Four highly specific compliance keywords will only match postings that happen to use those exact words, and many public job descriptions do not. Cut to 'grants, federal funding, compliance' and let the titles do the narrowing instead: 'grants administrator, grants manager, contracts administrator, fiscal compliance'. Add 'Santa Fe, Rio Rancho, Remote' to locations. Two runs a day is enough. The real gap is channel coverage: county, state and nonprofit employers post on their own government job portals rather than anywhere the scan reaches. Check those portals weekly for the agencies you would work for and track openings with Add role.

Everything is a C fit or below. VP of commercial for a cruise line, based Miami. Titles 'commercial director, revenue director'. Keywords 'revenue management, pricing'. Seniority 'Director'. I am seeing hotel revenue manager jobs three levels down.

Two things are pulling you down a level: a Director seniority setting and titles that read as mid-management. Set seniority to VP, and change titles to 'vice president commercial, SVP revenue, chief commercial officer'. Keep 'revenue management' but add 'net yield, distribution strategy, itinerary deployment, onboard revenue' so the scan reads for perishable inventory at portfolio scale. Add 'Fort Lauderdale, Remote' to locations. At this level the scan will return very little, which is expected, so run it twice a day and put your energy into the channel that fills these seats: search firms and direct conversations with commercial leaders at cruise and expedition operators.

All C fits. I am a clinical lab scientist in Madison moving into healthcare data analysis. Titles 'data analyst'. Keywords 'SQL, Power BI, Python'. Locations 'Madison'. I get generic analyst roles at insurance and marketing firms that want three years as an analyst.

Generic analyst titles hide the one thing that makes you competitive, which is that you already work inside a hospital. Change titles to 'healthcare data analyst, clinical informatics analyst, clinical data analyst' and add 'laboratory, Epic, HL7, turnaround time, HIPAA' to keywords. That flips your lab background from a gap into the qualification. Add 'Remote, Milwaukee' to locations. Three runs a day on that set. Health systems and specialty clinic networks run their own hiring pages and list there first, so check every system within an hour of Madison directly and use Add role for anything the scan missed.

Too few results, and the ones I get want ten years in consumer credit at a bank. Director of credit risk at a digital lender, New York. Titles 'director of credit risk'. Keywords 'CECL, scorecard, XGBoost, champion challenger'. Locations 'New York'.

One exact title plus four technical keywords is why volume is near zero. Broaden titles to 'director of credit risk, VP risk analytics, head of credit strategy, senior manager model risk'. Reduce keywords to 'credit risk, underwriting, loss forecasting' and let your resume carry the modeling detail. Add 'Remote' to locations, since risk analytics teams at digital lenders are widely distributed. Two runs a day. Then work the channel the scan cannot see: fintech lenders often hire through investor networks and referrals well before posting, so ask two people in your network which portfolios are rebuilding their decision engines.
Nothing is moving. 19 cards in Sourced, 2 in Applied since April. Production manager on a food packaging line in Cedar Rapids. I run the search most days out of habit.

Daily searching is filling the top of a funnel that has no bottom. Drop to two runs a day and set a rule that you research and apply to two roles before adding any new ones. Take the 19 and split them: the ones inside a 45-minute drive get a match report this week, the rest go to Closed. For the two in Applied since April, treat them as cold and follow up once by finding the plant's HR contact directly. Add 'plant manager, continuous improvement manager' to titles so you catch both the step up and the sideways move into a CI role.
High ghost risk. Most of my board is county and city IT postings that have been open since winter, some with no closing date. Director of IT for county government in Spokane.

Open-ended public sector postings are usually continuous recruitments, not ghosts, but they move on a budget calendar rather than a hiring urgency. Check each posting for a closing date; the ones without one belong at the back of the queue. Two searches a day is plenty here. The higher-value channel is watching the agencies themselves: council and commission agendas show when a technology position is authorized and funded, which is the real signal that a posting will convert. Add 'chief information officer, IT director, technology director' to titles and add neighboring counties and special-purpose authorities to locations.

All D fits. E-commerce merchandising manager in Los Angeles. Titles 'merchandising manager'. Keywords 'merchandising, retail, e-commerce'. Locations 'Los Angeles'. I keep getting in-store visual merchandising and buying assistant roles.

Bare 'merchandising' is a store-side word, which is why you are getting visual merchandising and assistant buyer results. Replace keywords with 'site merchandising, category taxonomy, site search, conversion rate, PIM, A/B testing' so the scan reads digital. Change titles to 'senior manager digital merchandising, e-commerce merchandising manager, category manager marketplace'. Add 'Remote' to locations, since digital merchandising roles are frequently remote. Three runs a day on that set. Direct-to-consumer brands almost always list a merchandising role at home before it reaches a board, so pick eight whose assortment resembles yours, check them weekly, and track finds with Add role.
Too few results. Warehouse systems analyst in Reno. Titles 'WMS analyst'. Keywords 'Manhattan Active, slotting, wave planning'. Locations 'Reno'. Most days the scan finds nothing new.

A vendor-specific keyword plus a niche title plus one small market is close to an empty query. Change titles to 'WMS analyst, warehouse systems analyst, business analyst distribution, fulfillment systems analyst'. Cut 'Manhattan Active' from keywords, since most postings say warehouse management system instead, and keep 'slotting, wave planning' plus add 'SQL, labor standards'. Add 'Sparks, Las Vegas, Salt Lake City, Remote' to locations. Two runs a day. Distribution employers cluster in a handful of logistics corridors and hire straight off their own websites, so identify the operators running buildings near you and watch those sites directly.

My board is full of engineering manager roles two levels below me. VP of engineering, 78 engineers, remote from Minneapolis. Titles 'engineering manager, director of engineering'. Seniority 'Director'.

The board is accurate to your settings, which is the problem. Set seniority to VP and drop 'engineering manager' from titles entirely. Use 'VP of engineering, SVP engineering, head of engineering' instead, and add 'multi-tenant, DORA, SRE, platform migration' to keywords so the scan reads for organizational scope rather than team lead work. Keep 'Remote' and add 'Minneapolis'. Two runs a day is right at this level. Executive engineering roles are also filled through search firms and board networks long before posting, so make sure two or three people who know your work are aware you are open.
Wrong locations and wrong roles. Tour operations manager in Phoenix. Locations set to 'Remote' only. I am getting remote travel agent and customer service roles, nothing operational.

Remote-only is steering you into contact center work, which is the largest category of remote travel jobs. Change locations to 'Phoenix, Scottsdale, Remote' so on-site and hybrid operations roles come back into range. Change titles to 'tour operations manager, director of operations travel, supplier performance manager' and add 'ground handling, supplier contracting, itinerary costing, crisis response' to keywords. That combination filters out agent roles cleanly. Three runs a day. Tour operators and expedition companies are small employers who rarely reach aggregators, so build a list of fifteen of them and check their careers pages monthly, adding anything live with Add role.

All C fits. Hospital pharmacist in Nashville targeting medical affairs and health-tech. Titles 'pharmacist'. Keywords 'clinical pharmacy, PharmD'. Locations 'Nashville'. Everything returned is retail and hospital staff pharmacist jobs.

The title 'pharmacist' will only ever return dispensing roles. Remove it. Use 'medical writer, medical science liaison, clinical content lead, clinical informatics' as titles instead, and change keywords to 'PharmD, antimicrobial stewardship, protocol development, medical affairs, literature evaluation'. That keeps your credential visible while pointing the scan at the industry side. Add 'Remote' to locations, since medical communications and health-tech roles are heavily remote. Three runs a day on the new set. Medical communications agencies and health-tech companies also list openings on their own sites well before aggregators pick them up, so check a dozen directly.

Too few results and high ghost risk on the rest. High school math teacher moving into fintech data analysis, Kansas City. Titles 'data scientist'. Keywords 'machine learning, Python, scikit-learn'. Locations 'Kansas City'.

You are aiming one rung too high, which shrinks volume and fills the little you get with roles that were never realistically open to you. Change titles to 'data analyst, analyst portfolio reporting, pricing analyst, business analyst' and drop 'data scientist'. Replace keywords with 'SQL, Python, statistics, reporting, credit'. Add 'Overland Park, Remote' to locations. Three runs a day. For a career changer the highest-yield channel is not a board at all: lenders, insurers and credit unions in your metro hire juniors through their own careers pages, and two informational conversations will beat twenty applications.
Nothing is moving. 15 cards, all Sourced, none applied. Controls and automation technician in Toledo. Titles 'controls technician'. I run the search when I get home and add whatever it finds.

Adding cards is not progress. Cap the search at two runs a day and commit to applying to two roles before you add another. Controls roles get filled fast, so the ones sitting in Sourced for more than a week are probably gone; check each posting is still live and Close the ones that are not. Add 'controls engineer, maintenance supervisor, automation technician' to titles so you catch both the engineering title and the promotion-shaped supervisor roles. Add 'Studio 5000, PLC, HMI, robotics' to keywords. Plants near you fill these roles off their own websites, often within days, so check them weekly.

All C and D fits. Senior policy analyst at a state housing agency in Sacramento. Titles 'policy analyst'. Keywords 'policy, research, analysis'. Locations 'Sacramento'. I am getting legislative aide and communications roles.

'Policy, research, analysis' matches almost any government posting, which is how communications roles are reaching you. Replace with 'program evaluation, housing policy, quantitative analysis, R, cost-benefit'. Change titles to 'senior research associate, manager research and evaluation, policy analyst, program evaluator' so both research organizations and agencies are in range. Add 'Remote' to locations, since research institutes and evaluation consultancies hire nationally. Three runs a day. Research organizations and think tanks recruit through academic mailing lists and their own announcements rather than job boards, so subscribe to two of those lists and track openings with Add role.

High ghost risk. Several retail VP postings on my board have been open for months and one recruiter told me the role was already filled internally. Director of store operations, Atlanta, 210 stores today.

At director and VP level, long-open retail postings are frequently placeholders while an internal candidate is being confirmed. Do not stop applying, but change the order of operations: before you apply, check whether the company's leadership page already shows someone in the seat, and look for whether the posting has been refreshed rather than reposted. Two searches a day is enough. Put the recovered time into the channel that decides these roles, which is people. Tell two retail recruiters you know exactly what you want, and add 'VP retail operations, director field operations' to titles.

Wrong locations. Director of last-mile delivery in Philadelphia. Locations set to 'Remote, Philadelphia'. Remote is returning freight brokerage sales and dispatcher jobs and burying the operational roles.

Remote in a field operations search is mostly matching brokerage and dispatch, which is why the good roles are buried. Drop Remote and set locations to 'Philadelphia, Newark, Baltimore, New York' so you cover the northeast corridor markets where parcel networks concentrate. Add 'VP last-mile, director of delivery operations' to titles and 'cost per stop, route density, delivery service partner, telematics' to keywords. Three runs a day is enough at that focus. Regional carriers and retailers standing up their own delivery networks advertise directly and rarely syndicate, so check eight of them weekly and track finds with Add role.
I have 34 cards and every single one came from Run job search. Nothing from anywhere else. Nothing has moved past Applied in two months. I work in operations management.

One channel producing a whole board is the problem. Aggregated scans surface roles after they have already been seen by hundreds of people, which is why your applications stall. Keep the search to two runs a day, then add three channels deliberately: pick fifteen employers you would actually work for and check their careers pages directly every week, ask four former colleagues what their companies are hiring for, and follow two industry newsletters that publish roles. Track everything you find with Add role so the board reflects the real mix. A board sourced from one channel will always convert at that channel's rate.

Too few results. I set target titles to one exact string, seniority to one level, and a single city, because I wanted precision. Now the search returns nothing most days and I have used all 10 runs proving it.

Precision at every filter at once multiplies into an empty set, and running the same empty query ten times cannot fix it. Loosen exactly one filter at a time so you learn which one is binding. Start with seniority, since it is usually the most brittle: add the level above and below. If volume is still low, add two adjacent titles rather than more keywords. Only widen location third. Use one run per change and stop when you get five to ten new roles a day, which is the volume you can actually act on. Precision belongs in which roles you apply to, not in what you see.
Everything is a C fit. My keywords are 'management, leadership, strategy, operations'. I thought broad keywords would give me more to choose from.

Those four words appear in nearly every posting ever written, so the scan has nothing to discriminate on and returns whatever is most common. Keywords should be the things only your kind of role would say. Replace all four with five or six concrete nouns from your own work: the systems you use, the metrics you own, the regulations or standards you work under, the unit of production you manage. Let target titles carry the level and let keywords carry the domain. Then run the search twice and compare the fit scores before and after. If fits do not improve, your keywords are still too generic.
Wrong locations. I put 'Remote' in Settings because I would prefer remote, but now everything on the board is remote and I am missing local roles I would happily take.

Remote is not a preference filter, it is an inclusion term, so on its own it excludes everything local. Put your city and the two or three nearest metros in the field alongside Remote. If the board then fills with remote roles that are a poor fit, the issue is that remote postings attract enormous volume in most fields, so tighten your titles rather than removing Remote. Run the search twice on the mixed setting and look at where the better fit scores land. That tells you which side of the split deserves your applications, rather than guessing at it.
High ghost risk on almost everything. How do I tell which postings are real before I spend an hour tailoring a resume?

Check three things in about two minutes each. First, open the posting from the card and see whether the employer's own careers page still lists it; aggregator copies outlive the original constantly. Second, look for a posted or refreshed date, and treat anything older than eight weeks with no update as low priority. Third, check whether the same title has been posted repeatedly this year, which usually means a comp or bar problem rather than a fake role. Order your board by that assessment and tailor only for the top of it. Two searches a day is enough once you are filtering this way.
Nothing is moving. 23 cards in Sourced, 3 in Applied for five weeks with no response. Hotel revenue management, Denver. I keep running the search instead of applying because applying feels worse.

Then change what the search is for. Two runs a day, and no new card gets added until two existing ones move to Applied or Closed. Of the 23, keep only the properties and groups you would actually join and Close the rest so the board reads honestly. On the three sitting in Applied, five weeks is past the point of a polite follow-up, so find the commercial leader at each on LinkedIn and send two sentences referencing something specific in the posting. Then widen slightly: add 'director of revenue strategy' to titles so you see roles worth the effort of a real application.

Too few results. Clinical operations, Columbus. Titles 'clinical operations manager'. Keywords 'Epic Cadence, infusion, chair time, Joint Commission'. Locations 'Columbus'. Some days there is nothing new at all.

Four keywords that narrow means the posting has to be written by someone doing your exact job. Trim to 'Epic, ambulatory, throughput' and let the titles do the filtering: 'clinical operations manager, director of ambulatory operations, operations manager healthcare, practice manager'. Widen locations with 'Dublin, Westerville, Dayton, Remote'. Two or three of your ten daily runs against that set will return more than the current filter does in a week. Then work the employer list yourself — eight health systems, infusion providers and home health companies within an hour of Columbus, checked weekly, with Add role for whatever the scan has not surfaced.
High ghost risk. Several staff engineer postings on my board have been open since January and one company has three nearly identical ledger roles listed. Payments backend, Austin.

Three near-identical listings usually means a pipeline requisition rather than three real seats, and January postings on an engineering team are often kept open to collect resumes. Check whether the company's own careers page shows the same three, and whether any engineer has joined that team recently. Deprioritize rather than skip them. Two searches a day is plenty. The better channel for senior payments work is people: engineers who have worked on ledgers know which teams are genuinely hiring, so ask two of them. Add 'principal engineer, engineering manager payments' to titles to widen the shape of what you see.

Every result is a C or D. Plant operations, Grand Rapids. Titles 'operations manager, production manager'. Keywords 'manufacturing, operations, lean'. Seniority 'Manager'. I run a 420-person plant and report to a COO.

Your seniority and titles both describe the job two levels below yours, so the scan is returning exactly what you asked for. Set seniority to Director and VP. Replace titles with 'plant manager, VP manufacturing operations, director of operations, general manager'. Change keywords from the generic three to 'OEE, scrap, IATF 16949, union, P&L' so the scan reads for site accountability rather than shop floor supervision. Two runs a day is enough at that level. Plant leadership roles also move through supplier and customer networks, so let two contacts at your OEM customers know you are open.
Too few results. Transit capital delivery, Portland. Titles 'transit capital program manager'. Keywords 'FTA, Small Starts, Primavera P6, NEPA'. Locations 'Portland'. Most runs return nothing.

Four agency-specific acronyms will only match postings written by someone in transit, and you are qualified for far more than transit. Cut keywords to 'capital program, federal grants, construction management' and broaden titles to 'capital programs director, senior project manager, program manager infrastructure'. That opens water, airport, port and facilities work where your grant and schedule skills transfer directly. Add 'Vancouver, Salem, Remote' to locations. Two runs a day. Public agencies post on their own job portals rather than anywhere a scan reaches, so check the portals of the four largest authorities near you weekly and use Add role.

Wrong locations. Store operations lead in Charlotte, moving into analytics. Locations 'Charlotte, Remote'. The remote results are all senior analyst roles wanting five years, and the local ones are store manager jobs.

You have two mismatched funnels in one setting. Remote analytics roles skew senior because companies hire remotely for experience, and Charlotte results skew store-side because that is what your titles say. Fix the titles first: 'retail operations analyst, business analyst, operations analyst' with 'store manager' nowhere in the field. Then keep Charlotte and add 'Raleigh, Greenville' rather than Remote, because a first analytics role is much easier to get where someone can meet you. Three runs a day. Employers with distribution centers and store support offices near you are the best target; check their careers pages directly.
High ghost risk. Two SVP supply chain roles on my board have been listed for four months and both are through the same recruiter. Memphis, six DCs today.

At SVP level, a role that has been open four months through a search firm is usually real but stuck, either on comp or on a founder who cannot decide. That is worth a conversation but not a cold application. Contact the recruiter directly and ask what has changed since the search opened; the answer tells you whether it is worth your time. Keep the scan at two runs a day. Executive supply chain seats are filled through search firms and board introductions, so the highest-value action is making sure three retained recruiters in your sector know your name and your number.
Nothing is moving. 26 cards, most in Sourced, 5 in Applied with two rejections. Senior product manager, Seattle. I add roles faster than I apply to them.

Cut sourcing to two runs a day and set a floor of two real applications a week. Product applications fail on volume more than any other function, so a tailored application to six well-chosen roles will beat thirty generic ones. Take your 26 and keep only the ones where you can name the area you would own; Close the rest. For the two rejections, look at whether they were both first-time PM management roles, because that is the gap the market is reading. If so, add 'principal product manager' and 'group product manager' to titles alongside the director roles.
All C and D fits. Director of nursing, post-acute, Baltimore. Titles 'nurse manager, clinical manager'. Keywords 'nursing, healthcare, patient care'. Seniority 'Manager'.

Your settings describe a floor nurse manager role, which is two levels below where you sit. Set seniority to Director and VP, and replace titles with 'director of nursing, VP clinical operations, chief nursing officer, regional director of clinical services'. Change keywords to 'skilled nursing, CMS five-star, survey, agency spend, MDS' so the scan reads for post-acute leadership specifically rather than any clinical job. Two runs a day is right at this level. Multi-site operators also recruit through nursing leadership networks and state associations, so make sure two peers in those circles know you are looking.
Wrong locations. Network planning analyst, Chicago. Locations 'Chicago, Remote'. Remote is returning logistics coordinator and travel agency jobs and none of them are airline planning.

Airline network planning happens at headquarters, so Remote is only bringing in unrelated travel and logistics work. Drop Remote and set locations to 'Chicago, Dallas, Atlanta, Denver, Minneapolis', which is where carriers concentrate. If you would not relocate, keep Chicago alone and accept lower volume, because widening on Remote will not produce a single real match. Add 'schedule planning, capacity planning, revenue management analyst' to titles so adjacent commercial roles at the same carriers come into range. Two runs a day. Carriers, coach operators and rail companies keep their openings on in-house boards, so check those weekly.
Too few results. Consumer lending compliance, Salt Lake City. Titles 'compliance manager consumer lending'. Keywords 'TILA, Reg Z, ECOA, UDAAP, NMLS, fair lending'. Locations 'Salt Lake City'.

You have six regulation names in the keyword field, and a posting only has to skip one of them to drop out of your results. Keep 'fair lending' and 'consumer lending'; the rest belongs on your resume, not in the filter. Open titles up to 'compliance manager, compliance director, regulatory affairs manager, senior compliance officer'. Add 'Remote' to locations, since nonbank lending compliance is unusually remote-friendly and Salt Lake City alone is a thin market for it. Three runs a day is the most this is worth. Fintech lenders staff compliance in bursts and fill through referral, so tell two people in your regulatory network what kind of program you want to run next.
Wrong locations. Quality engineer, Greenville SC. Locations 'South Carolina'. I am getting Charleston and Columbia roles that are two hours away, and I think I am missing things in the upstate.

A statewide setting spreads volume across markets you cannot commute to while giving no extra weight to the ones you can. Replace 'South Carolina' with 'Greenville, Spartanburg, Anderson, Greer' and add 'Charlotte' only if you would drive it. While you are there, add 'supplier quality engineer, quality manager' to titles so you catch both the sideways and upward moves, and add 'SPC, AS9100, PPAP, GD&T' to keywords so the scan reads for precision manufacturing rather than any quality role. Three runs a day. Aerospace and medical device suppliers in the upstate rarely reach aggregators at all, so go to their own sites.
Nothing is moving. 31 cards on the board, 21 still in Sourced, one interview that went nowhere. Head of customer success, Boston.

Twenty-one untouched cards is a research problem, not a sourcing problem. Cap the search at two runs a day and work the pile down: for each, decide in five minutes whether the company's retention problem is one you would want, and Close the ones that are not. Leadership searches convert on conversations, so for the top five, find someone one degree away and ask about the team before you apply. On the interview that stalled, ask directly for feedback; at your level people usually give it, and it will tell you whether the gap is scale, sector, or board exposure.

All C and D fits. Transportation manager, Fort Worth. Titles 'logistics manager, supply chain manager'. Keywords 'logistics, supply chain, warehouse, distribution'. I run outbound truckload and LTL for two DCs.

Those keywords describe the whole industry, so the scan cannot tell your function apart from planning, warehousing or procurement. Replace with 'truckload, LTL, carrier procurement, TMS, on-time in full, cost per mile'. Change titles to 'transportation manager, director of transportation, logistics manager outbound' so the scan reads transportation specifically rather than supply chain generally. Keep seniority at Manager and add Director. Three runs a day on that set. Distributors, 3PLs and manufacturers in your metro post transportation roles on their own careers pages first, so check a dozen of them weekly and use Add role.
High ghost risk. Four county and nonprofit grants postings on my board have been open since February with no closing date. Albuquerque.

Continuous recruitment is normal in public and nonprofit hiring, so those four are probably real but slow rather than fake. Check each for a stated closing or first-review date; where there is none, apply once and move on rather than waiting. Two searches a day is enough. The stronger channel for grants work is the funding cycle itself: organizations hire administrators right after a large award lands, so watch award announcements from the state and from federal agencies in your program areas, then approach those organizations directly. Track anything you find that way with Add role.

Too few results. VP commercial, cruise, Miami. Titles 'VP commercial cruise'. Keywords 'net yield, itinerary deployment, cruise revenue management'. Locations 'Miami'. The scan returns almost nothing.

Cruise is a small industry and you have filtered to one word inside it, so near-zero results are arithmetic rather than a settings mistake. Broaden the industry instead of the level: titles should read 'VP commercial, SVP revenue, chief commercial officer, VP revenue management', and keywords should read 'yield, pricing, distribution, perishable inventory' so hotel, airline and tour operators come into range. Add 'Fort Lauderdale, Remote' to locations. Two runs a day. The rest of this search happens off any board, through search firms and direct approaches to commercial leaders at operators you would join.
Nothing is moving. 17 cards in Sourced, no applications. Clinical lab scientist in Madison targeting healthcare analytics. I keep telling myself I need one more certification first.

The certificate is not the blocker, the application count is. Two searches a day, and pick three roles this week to apply to properly. For each, get a match report first so you know which gaps to address in the resume and which to leave for the interview. Close anything in Sourced that wants five years as an analyst, because that is not the door that opens for you. The door that does open is internal and adjacent: your own health system's analytics team, and the systems nearby, where your lab and Epic experience is the reason to hire you rather than a gap.
All C fits. Director of credit risk, New York. Titles 'risk manager, analytics manager'. Keywords 'risk, analytics, modeling'. Seniority 'Manager'. I own strategy for a $3.1B book.

The settings are describing an analyst manager, so that is what comes back. Set seniority to Director and VP. Change titles to 'director of credit risk, VP risk analytics, head of credit strategy'. Replace the three generic keywords with 'credit risk, underwriting, loss forecasting, portfolio, scorecard' so the scan reads for lending rather than any risk function. Add 'Remote' to locations. Two runs a day at that level. Credit leadership roles also surface through capital providers and warehouse lenders who see which portfolios are hiring, so use those relationships rather than relying on the board alone.
Wrong locations. Production manager, Cedar Rapids. Locations 'Iowa, Remote'. Remote gives me nothing usable and Iowa is returning roles four hours away.

Production management is on-site by definition, so Remote is pure noise here; delete it. Replace 'Iowa' with 'Cedar Rapids, Iowa City, Marion, Waterloo' and add 'Des Moines' only if you would relocate. Then use the space you have freed on titles: add 'plant manager, continuous improvement manager, operations manager' so you see both the step up and the sideways move. Add 'SMED, changeover, OEE, SQF' to keywords so food and packaging plants surface ahead of general manufacturing. Three runs a day. The plants in those four markets recruit off their own websites, so check them weekly.
Too few results. County IT director, Spokane. Titles 'chief information officer'. Keywords 'public sector, ERP, cybersecurity governance, ITIL'. Locations 'Spokane'.

One title at the top of the ladder plus a small market is why volume is near zero. Broaden titles to 'chief information officer, IT director, director of technology, deputy CIO' and cut keywords to 'public sector, ERP, cybersecurity'. Add 'Spokane Valley, Coeur d'Alene, Tri-Cities, Remote' to locations, and consider special-purpose authorities, ports, utilities and school districts, which hire the same skill set. Two runs a day. Public sector technology roles are posted on agency job portals rather than anywhere a scan reaches, so check the portals for your region's agencies weekly and use Add role.
Nothing is moving. 24 cards, 18 in Sourced, 4 Applied, no responses in six weeks. E-commerce merchandising, Los Angeles.

Six weeks of silence on four applications usually means the applications were generic, not that the roles were closed. Drop to two searches a day and rebuild the four: for each, tailor to the specific catalog problem the posting describes, whether that is taxonomy, search relevance or markdown timing. Close the Sourced cards from brands whose assortment you do not understand, because those applications will not land either. Then add a second channel: pick ten brands whose category structure you admire, check their careers pages weekly, and reach out to their merchandising leads directly rather than waiting for a posting.
High ghost risk. Three WMS analyst postings on my board have been live since spring and one has been reposted twice. Reno.

A twice-reposted systems role usually means the employer wants a configuration specialist and keeps getting ticket-desk applicants, which is good news for you if your resume leads with what you have configured rather than what you have supported. Apply, but lead with the pick-path and wave changes. Check each posting against the employer's own careers page first and Close any that no longer appear. Two searches a day. Distribution operators also hire systems analysts through the vendor and integrator community, so let two contacts on the implementation side know you are open.
Too few results. VP of engineering, remote from Minneapolis. Titles 'VP of engineering'. Keywords 'multi-tenant SaaS, DORA, SRE, error budgets, platform migration'. Locations 'Remote'.

Five engineering-culture keywords will only match postings written by someone fluent in the same vocabulary, which is a small subset even of good companies. Cut to 'SaaS, engineering leadership, platform' and let the title carry the level. Add 'SVP engineering, head of engineering, CTO' to titles. Keep Remote and add 'Minneapolis' so local companies surface too. Two runs a day; at this level the board will never be busy, and that is fine. The rest of the search is relationship work: search firms and two or three founders or CEOs who know your delivery record.
All C and D fits. Tour operations manager, Phoenix. Titles 'operations manager'. Keywords 'operations, travel, customer service'. I manage 340 departures a year across nine countries.

'Operations' and 'customer service' together are pulling call center and retail management, which is why nothing scores well. Replace keywords with 'tour operations, ground handling, supplier contracting, itinerary, DMC, crisis response'. Change titles to 'tour operations manager, director of operations travel, supplier performance manager, operations manager guided travel'. That combination is narrow enough to filter out service roles and broad enough to catch adjacent event and expedition operators. Three runs a day. Tour and expedition companies are small and rarely reach aggregators, so build a list of twenty and check their careers pages monthly.
Nothing is moving. 20 cards in Sourced, zero applications. Hospital pharmacist in Nashville targeting medical affairs. I am not sure I am qualified for any of them so I keep looking.

Looking is how you avoid finding out. Two searches a day, and apply to three this week regardless of how ready you feel. Run a match report on each first; for a clinician moving into industry, the gaps are almost always agency process and style rather than science, and those are learnable in a way the science is not. Close anything asking for years of agency experience and keep the roles that say they hire clinicians directly. Medical communications agencies hire off their own sites, and many will talk to a PharmD who writes well before a posting exists.
Wrong locations. Math teacher moving into fintech analytics, Kansas City. Locations 'Remote'. Every remote analyst role wants two years of professional experience I do not have.

Remote hiring selects for people who need no ramp, which is the opposite of your situation. Replace 'Remote' with 'Kansas City, Overland Park, Lenexa, Independence' so local employers who can meet you come into range; a first analytics job is far easier to get in person. Keep titles at 'data analyst, analyst portfolio reporting, pricing analyst' and drop anything senior. Three runs a day. Then use the channel that actually converts for career changers: banks, credit unions, insurers and lenders in your metro, approached directly, plus two informational conversations with people who made the same move.
Too few results. Controls technician, Toledo. Titles 'controls and automation technician'. Keywords 'Allen-Bradley, Studio 5000, FANUC, FactoryTalk, arc flash'. Locations 'Toledo'.

Studio 5000, FactoryTalk and FANUC are vendor names. A plant that writes 'PLC programming' in its posting will never reach you, and most of them do. Reduce keywords to 'PLC, HMI, robotics'. Open titles to 'controls technician, controls engineer, automation technician, maintenance supervisor' so both the engineering title and the supervisor step are in range. Add 'Perrysburg, Bowling Green, Findlay, Monroe' to locations. Two of your ten daily runs is plenty at that width. The faster channel is direct: plants in that corridor advertise in-house and hire the same week a line goes down, so check eight of them weekly and enter what you find with Add role.
High ghost risk. Two research organizations on my board have had the same senior researcher posting open since last year. Sacramento, housing policy.

Research institutes often keep a senior posting open continuously because they hire when funding lands rather than on a schedule, so those are worth an application but not repeated hope. Contact the research director directly and ask what would need to be true for the position to be filled this year; funding-driven hiring rewards being known before the money arrives. Two searches a day. Also add the channel that carries this field: academic and policy mailing lists, and the convenings where these organizations recruit. Track openings from either with Add role rather than waiting for the scan.

All C fits. Director of store operations, Atlanta. Titles 'store manager, district manager'. Keywords 'retail, stores, customer service'. Seniority 'Director'. I run 210 stores and a $92M expense budget.

The titles in your settings are the jobs of the people three levels below you, and they are dragging every result down. Replace with 'VP retail operations, director of field operations, SVP stores, director of retail operations'. Change keywords to 'multi-unit, labor model, shrink, four-wall margin, district managers' so the scan reads for fleet accountability. Set seniority to Director and VP. Two runs a day. Retail leadership at this level moves through recruiters and peer referral well before postings, so make sure two specialist retail recruiters have a current picture of what you want.
Nothing is moving. 29 cards, 22 in Sourced, and I have applied to three roles in two months. Director of last-mile, Philadelphia.

Twenty-two untouched cards means the board is a reading list, not a pipeline. Two searches a day, and no new cards until three move. Sort the 22 by whether the company actually runs its own delivery network, because the ones brokering it will not value your cost per stop work. Close the rest. For the three applications, follow up with the operations leader directly rather than the recruiter. Then add the channel this field runs on: retailers standing up delivery, regional carriers and delivery partner networks, approached through people you have worked with before.
I used all 10 searches today and got the same 4 roles every time. What am I doing wrong?

Running the same query repeatedly returns the same results, because the boards do not change hour to hour. Treat the daily allowance as ten different questions, not ten attempts at one. A good pattern is three runs in the morning on three distinct title sets, one run after a location change, and the rest held in reserve for when you actually change a filter. If a run produces nothing new, change something in Settings before running again. Once you find a filter set that returns five to ten usable roles a day, two runs a day will hold it and the rest of your time goes to applying.
My board is full of roles from staffing agencies and job aggregators rather than actual employers. Fit scores are all over the place.

Agency postings are often the same underlying job listed several times with the details sanded off, which is why the fit scores are inconsistent. Two habits fix most of it. First, before adding effort to a card, open the posting and see whether you can identify the actual employer; if you cannot, treat it as low priority. Second, when you can identify them, go to that employer's careers page and apply there instead, because a direct application beats an agency submission almost every time. Keep the scan at two runs a day and put the recovered time into building a list of employers you would work for.
I want to relocate in about six months but I do not want to waste the next six months applying somewhere I cannot start yet. How should I set the search up?

Run two searches in parallel rather than blending them. Keep your current metro in locations as the primary set, because those are the roles you can actually take now. Then once a week, change locations to the target metro alone, run two searches, and add the results with Add role so the destination market builds up on the board without polluting your daily results. What you learn from that weekly pass is what the target market pays, which titles it uses, and which employers hire your profile, all of which shapes the resume you will need. Switch the primary set over when you are eight weeks out.
Every posting the search finds asks for one more year of experience than I have. It happens across three different title variations.

A consistent one-year gap across three titles is a level signal, not a coincidence: you are searching one rung above where the market currently places you. Do two things at once. Keep a small number of stretch roles, no more than a quarter of what you apply to, and add the tier below to your titles so the realistic roles come back into view. Then check whether the gap is real or presentational, because stated years often stand in for a specific responsibility. If you have that responsibility and not the years, say so explicitly in the application rather than hoping the resume implies it.
The fit scores on my board are all in the fifties and sixties. Nothing scores high enough that I feel confident applying.

A board that clusters in the fifties usually means your filters are one notch off, not that you are unqualified. Look at what the mid-scoring roles have in common; if they all miss on the same requirement, that requirement belongs in your keywords so the scan stops surfacing roles that need it. If they miss on different things each time, your titles are too broad. Also worth saying plainly: a high score is not the threshold for applying. Roles in the sixties where the gap is a system you could learn are worth an application; roles in the eighties where the gap is a license are not.
I am getting good results but I have no time to act on them. Fifteen new cards a day is more than I can handle.

Fifteen a day is a sourcing rate for a full-time search, so tighten until the volume matches the hours you actually have. One search run a day rather than ten, and narrow the filter that costs you least: usually location, by removing the metro you would take but not prefer. Aim for three to five new cards a day, which is what one evening can properly research. Then protect the pipeline rather than the top of the funnel; two well-tailored applications a week from a small board will beat fifteen cards a day that nobody reads.
How do I decide which roles are worth a tailored resume when everything on my board looks roughly similar?

Rank by three things and tailor only the top of the list. First, whether you can name the specific problem the posting is trying to solve, because if you cannot, your application will read as generic no matter how much you tailor it. Second, whether the posting was published or refreshed in the last month. Third, whether you know anyone one degree away from the team. Roles that clear all three deserve a tailored resume and a cover letter; roles that clear two deserve a tailored resume; the rest deserve either a quick application or a move to Closed.
High ghost risk. Six resort revenue roles on my board are at the same management group and three have been open all season. Denver, hotel revenue management.

One group with six open revenue seats is either genuinely rebuilding or running an evergreen pipeline, and the season timing tells you which: resort groups post before ski season and hold them open until they fill. Apply to the two best-matched and skip the rest, since one recruiter is reading all six anyway. Check the group's own careers page for posted dates before you spend effort. Two searches a day. Then widen the employer set so one company stops dominating the board: add hotel management companies, vacation ownership operators and pricing software vendors as target employers you check directly, tracking finds with Add role.
Wrong locations. Clinical operations manager, Columbus. Locations 'Ohio'. I am getting Cleveland and Toledo roles that are two hours each way, which crowds out anything local.

Statewide is spreading your daily results across markets you cannot commute to. Replace 'Ohio' with 'Columbus, Dublin, Westerville, Delaware, Newark' so the results are actually actionable. Add 'Remote' only if you would take a system-level role that travels, since most ambulatory operations work is on-site. While you are in Settings, add 'director of ambulatory operations, operations manager home infusion' to titles so both the step up and the adjacent care setting come into range. Three runs a day at that geography. Health systems and infusion providers in central Ohio list openings in-house ahead of the boards, so check eight of them weekly.
All C fits. Staff backend engineer, payments, Austin. Titles 'software engineer, backend engineer'. Keywords 'Python, SQL, AWS'. Seniority '—'. Everything returned is mid-level product engineering.

Two generic titles, three generic cloud keywords and a blank seniority field is a specification for mid-level results. Fill seniority in with Principal and Senior. Titles become 'staff software engineer, principal engineer, senior staff engineer'. Then swap 'Python, SQL, AWS' for the vocabulary only your kind of work uses: 'ledger, payments, Kafka, distributed systems, Go'. Expect the board to shrink sharply and the fit scores on what survives to climb. Three of your ten daily runs is the right budget for this search. Payments infrastructure teams often open a role internally and fill it before any aggregator sees it, so pick a dozen companies, check them directly, and use Add role.
Too few results. Plant operations director, Grand Rapids. Titles 'VP manufacturing operations'. Keywords 'multi-site, IATF 16949, injection molding, union'. Locations 'Grand Rapids'.

One executive title plus four narrow keywords plus one city is close to an empty set, and 'multi-site' in particular will only match postings that happen to use that phrase. Keep 'IATF 16949' and drop the other three. Add 'plant manager, director of operations, general manager' to titles so you see the roles that are a lateral move as well as the step up. Add 'Holland, Kalamazoo, Lansing, Muskegon' to locations. Two runs a day. Suppliers in west Michigan recruit through plant leadership networks and their own sites rather than boards, so work both directly.
Nothing is moving. 18 cards in Sourced, two applications since spring. Transit capital delivery, Portland. Public agency hiring is slow and I think I have stopped trying.

Public hiring is slow, which is an argument for more applications in flight, not fewer. Two searches a day and three applications this month, treating each as a form-filling exercise rather than a creative one, because agency screening is about matching stated minimum qualifications precisely. Go through the 18 and check each against its minimum qualifications; Close anything where you miss a hard requirement like a license, since agency screening will not exercise judgment about it. For the rest, answer every supplemental question in the agency's own language. That single habit moves more public applications forward than anything else.
High ghost risk. Several analyst roles on my board have been listed for months at large retailers, and I suspect they are pipeline postings. Charlotte, moving from store operations into analytics.

Large retailers do run evergreen analyst requisitions, and they are genuinely worth applying to once, because they convert when a team suddenly has a vacancy. Apply, then move on rather than waiting. Check the employer careers page for a posted date and skip anything with no date and no closing. Two searches a day. Your stronger channel is internal and lateral: the analytics team at your own employer already knows your work, and district or regional analyst roles are frequently filled by store operators who taught themselves SQL. Ask your district manager directly who owns that hiring.
Too few results. VP supply chain, Memphis. Titles 'SVP supply chain, chief supply chain officer'. Keywords 'network design, S&OP, cost per case, WMS'. Locations 'Memphis'.

At the top of a function, low volume is normal, but you have narrowed further than the market allows. Add 'VP supply chain, VP operations, head of supply chain' to titles so you catch the level you already hold as well as the step up. Cut keywords to 'supply chain, distribution, S&OP'. Add 'Nashville, Atlanta, Dallas, Remote' to locations, since executives are hired across a region rather than a metro. Two runs a day. The rest is not a board problem: retained search firms handle most of these seats, so make sure three of them in consumer and industrial supply chain know you.
Wrong locations. Senior product manager, Seattle. Locations 'Remote'. The remote results are enormous in volume and mostly companies I have never heard of, and I am missing Seattle roles.

Remote-only in product returns the highest-volume, lowest-signal segment of the market. Add 'Seattle, Bellevue, Redmond' alongside Remote so local companies come back into range, and tighten the titles so the remote half is filtered by level rather than by luck: 'senior product manager, principal product manager, director of product'. Add 'B2B, platform, workflow automation' to keywords so consumer app roles drop away. Three runs a day. Then use the channel that works in a concentrated tech market: people. Two conversations with product leaders in Seattle will surface roles before they are posted anywhere.
Too few results. Director of nursing, post-acute, Baltimore. Titles 'director of nursing'. Keywords 'skilled nursing, CMS five-star, MDS, PointClickCare, agency spend'. Locations 'Baltimore'.

Five specialist keywords means the posting has to come from an operator who writes like one, and plenty are drafted by corporate recruiters who use none of that vocabulary. Keep 'skilled nursing' and delete the other four. Titles should read 'director of nursing, VP clinical operations, regional director of clinical services, chief nursing officer'. Extend locations to 'Columbia, Annapolis, Frederick, southern Pennsylvania'. Three daily runs out of your ten will cover a field that size. Multi-site operators keep vacancies live on their own websites year-round instead of pushing them to boards, so choose eight in your region, check them weekly, and add what you find with Add role.
Nothing is moving. 12 cards, all Sourced, no applications. Airline network planning, Chicago. There are so few roles that I am saving them for when my resume is perfect.

In a field this small, waiting is the expensive choice, because the twelve roles in front of you may be most of what appears this year. Two searches a day and apply to the best three this week with the resume you have; a match report will tell you the one or two gaps worth addressing in the cover note. Close anything requiring a fleet type or system you have never touched. Then treat the small market as an advantage: network planning teams are ten people, so the person who would hire you is findable and worth a direct message before a role opens.
High ghost risk. Two compliance manager postings on my board have been open since January at the same fintech, and the job description changed slightly between versions. Salt Lake City.

A description that changed between postings usually means the employer is still deciding what the role is, which is worth knowing before you invest. Apply to the current version and reference the scope explicitly in your note, because a candidate who names the ambiguity often helps them resolve it. Check the company careers page to confirm which version is live. Two searches a day. Meanwhile, build a second channel: lending compliance is a small community, so let two people who run testing programs know what you are looking for, and watch for enforcement news, which reliably precedes hiring.
Too few results. Quality engineer, Greenville SC. Titles 'quality engineer aerospace'. Keywords 'AS9100, SPC, PPAP, gauge R&R, PC-DMIS'. Locations 'Greenville'.

An industry-qualified title and five technical keywords over-filter the same search twice. Set titles to 'quality engineer, supplier quality engineer, quality manager' and let the keywords carry the industry: keep 'AS9100, SPC' and drop 'PPAP, gauge R&R, PC-DMIS'. Add 'Spartanburg, Anderson, Greer, Charlotte' to locations. Two runs a day is the right budget here; the other eight are better spent elsewhere. Upstate precision manufacturers are small enough that hiring travels by supplier relationship and a note on the company website, never an aggregator. Build a list of fifteen machining, aerospace and medical device suppliers within an hour and check it monthly, adding live openings with Add role.
Wrong locations. Head of customer success, Boston. Locations 'Remote'. I am getting individual contributor CSM roles at tiny startups and missing the Boston leadership roles I actually want.

Remote-only in customer success skews heavily toward individual contributor roles, because that is what distributed teams hire most of. Add 'Boston, Cambridge, Waltham' alongside Remote. Then filter by level rather than geography: set seniority to Director and VP, and change titles to 'VP customer success, head of customer success, director of customer success, head of retention'. Add 'gross retention, net revenue retention, Gainsight, expansion' to keywords so the scan reads for ownership rather than account management. Two runs a day. At this level, referrals through your own network of software executives will outperform the board.
Nothing is moving. 25 cards, 19 Sourced. Transportation manager, Fort Worth. I add roles every evening and never get to the applications.

The evening habit is the problem, because sourcing is easier than applying and it feels like the same thing. Move the search to two runs in the morning and give the evening entirely to applications. Take the 19 and keep only the ones within your commute where the freight profile matches what you have run; Close the rest so the board reflects reality. Then set a weekly floor of three applications. Distributors and 3PLs move fast on transportation hires, so a card that sits a week is usually gone; treating them as perishable will change how you work the board.

All C and D fits. Grants administrator, Albuquerque. Titles 'administrator, program manager'. Keywords 'administration, compliance, reporting'. I am getting office manager and program coordinator roles.

Those titles and keywords describe general administration, which is why office manager roles are matching. Replace titles with 'grants administrator, grants manager, contracts administrator, fiscal compliance officer' and keywords with 'federal grants, Uniform Guidance, subrecipient, allowability, drawdown'. That combination cannot match an office manager posting. Add 'Santa Fe, Rio Rancho, Remote' to locations. Three runs a day. Then cover the channel the scan cannot see: county, state, tribal and nonprofit employers post on their own portals, so check those weekly for the organizations you would work for and use Add role.
Nothing is moving. 14 cards, mostly Sourced, one conversation that went quiet. VP commercial for a cruise line, Miami.

Fourteen cards is a normal board at your level, so the issue is conversion rather than volume. Two searches a day, and convert the board into conversations rather than applications: at VP level, an application without an internal advocate rarely moves. For each of the top five, find someone who has worked with the commercial team and ask for a real introduction. On the conversation that went quiet, go back once with something specific and useful, such as a view on their deployment or distribution mix, rather than a check-in. That is what restarts executive conversations.
Too few results. Clinical lab scientist targeting analytics, Madison. Titles 'healthcare data analyst'. Keywords 'Epic Beaker, HL7, Levey-Jennings, autoverification'. Locations 'Madison'.

Epic Beaker, HL7, Levey-Jennings and autoverification are bench vocabulary. The manager writing an analytics req has never typed any of them. Replace all four with 'SQL, Power BI, healthcare, quality measures'. Widen titles to 'healthcare data analyst, clinical informatics analyst, data analyst, quality analyst' and add 'Milwaukee, Remote' to locations. Two runs a day is all this will support out of the ten available. The channel that matters more for you is internal: health systems fill analytics seats from inside at a high rate, so apply into your own organisation's openings and ask that analytics team directly what makes a lab-side candidate credible. That conversation is worth more than volume.
High ghost risk. A director of credit risk posting on my board has been open eight months and the company has posted three variations of it. New York.

Eight months and three variations usually means the company cannot decide between a modeler and a strategist, and is waiting for a candidate who reads as both. That is a real opportunity if your resume leads with the scorecard rebuild and the governance documentation together rather than choosing one. Apply to the most recent variation only. Check whether the company's careers page still lists all three. Two searches a day. Meanwhile, work the channel where these roles are decided: capital providers and warehouse lenders see which lenders are rebuilding credit, and they make introductions.
Too few results. Production manager, Cedar Rapids. Titles 'production manager food'. Keywords 'SMED, OEE, SQF, tier board, autonomous maintenance'. Locations 'Cedar Rapids'.

A qualified title and five continuous improvement terms over-filter on both axes at once. Titles become 'production manager, plant manager, operations manager, continuous improvement manager'. Keep 'SQF, OEE' and drop 'SMED, tier board, autonomous maintenance'. Add 'Iowa City, Marion, Waterloo, Dubuque' to locations. Two runs a day is enough now, and the remaining eight are not worth burning. Food and packaging plants in eastern Iowa fill roles through plant-manager networks and their own websites long before an aggregator lists them, so pick twelve within an hour's drive, check them monthly, and add openings with Add role.
All C and D fits. County IT director, Spokane. Titles 'IT manager, systems manager'. Keywords 'IT, technology, infrastructure'. Seniority 'Manager'. I run a 64-person department and a $31M budget.

Your settings describe an infrastructure manager, so that is the level coming back. Set seniority to Director and VP, and change titles to 'chief information officer, IT director, director of technology, deputy CIO'. Replace the three broad keywords with 'public sector, ERP, cybersecurity governance, records retention' so the scan reads for government technology leadership rather than any IT job. Two runs a day. Public sector executive roles are posted on agency portals and often advertised through professional associations for government technology leaders, so watch both directly and use Add role.
Too few results. E-commerce merchandising, Los Angeles. Titles 'senior manager digital merchandising'. Keywords 'PIM, faceted search, taxonomy, GA4, headless'. Locations 'Los Angeles'.

'Headless' and 'faceted search' are words engineers use and merchandising postings rarely do, so they are silently excluding most of your market. Keep 'PIM, taxonomy' and drop the rest. Broaden titles to 'digital merchandising manager, e-commerce manager, category manager, senior manager site merchandising'. Add 'Remote, Orange County' to locations. Three runs a day. Then work the employer list directly: pick fifteen direct-to-consumer and multi-brand retailers whose catalogs resemble yours, check their careers pages every week, and track finds with Add role so the board is not dependent on one scan.
All C and D fits. Warehouse systems analyst, Reno. Titles 'analyst'. Keywords 'analyst, reporting, data'. Locations 'Reno, Remote'. I get marketing and finance analyst roles.

The word 'analyst' on its own matches every function in every industry, which is exactly what you are seeing. Change titles to 'warehouse systems analyst, WMS analyst, business analyst distribution, fulfillment systems analyst' and keywords to 'WMS, slotting, wave planning, SQL, labor standards'. Drop Remote, since warehouse systems work is on-site by nature, and add 'Sparks, Fernley, Las Vegas'. Three runs a day. Distribution operators cluster along a few corridors and do most of their hiring on site-level pages, so identify the buildings near you and check those employers directly.
Wrong locations. VP of engineering, Minneapolis-based but remote. Locations 'Minneapolis'. Almost nothing appears, and I would work remotely for a company anywhere.

If you would work remotely, a single metro is throwing away most of your market, since engineering leadership at this level is heavily distributed. Add 'Remote' to locations alongside Minneapolis. Keep the title list tight so remote volume does not overwhelm you: 'VP of engineering, SVP engineering, head of engineering, CTO', with seniority set to VP. Two runs a day. The board will still be thin, which is normal at executive level, so put the majority of your effort into search firms who place engineering executives and into two or three founders who would vouch for your delivery record.
Too few results. Tour operations manager, Phoenix. Titles 'tour operations manager'. Keywords 'DMC, ground handling, itinerary costing, supplier scorecard'. Locations 'Phoenix'.

That is one job title in a small industry in a city that is not a hub for it, which explains the empty results. Broaden titles to 'operations manager travel, director of operations, supplier performance manager, program operations manager' and cut keywords to 'tour operations, supplier contracting, travel'. Add 'Remote, Denver, Scottsdale' to locations, since many tour operators run operations teams remotely. Two runs a day. The decisive channel here is a target list: twenty tour, expedition and incentive travel companies whose careers pages you check monthly, tracked with Add role.
Wrong locations. Hospital pharmacist in Nashville targeting industry roles. Locations 'Nashville'. Medical affairs and health-tech barely exist here and I would work remotely.

Then say so in Settings, because a single metro is hiding most of the market you want. Add 'Remote' to locations alongside Nashville; medical communications, medical affairs and health-tech content roles are unusually remote-friendly. Keep titles pointed away from dispensing: 'medical writer, clinical content lead, medical science liaison, clinical informatics'. Add 'PharmD, stewardship, protocol, literature evaluation' to keywords. Three runs a day. Agencies and health-tech companies recruit clinicians continuously through their own channels, so build a list of fifteen and check them monthly rather than relying on the scan.
All C and D fits. Math teacher moving into fintech analytics, Kansas City. Titles 'analyst'. Keywords 'data, analysis, Excel'. I am getting accounts payable and administrative roles.

'Analyst' plus 'Excel' is the signature of clerical postings, which is why administrative roles are matching. Change titles to 'data analyst, pricing analyst, portfolio reporting analyst, business analyst' and keywords to 'SQL, Python, statistics, regression, credit'. That combination will not match an accounts payable posting. Keep locations at your metro plus the nearby suburbs rather than Remote, since a first analytics role is easier to win locally. Three runs a day. Then approach lenders, insurers and credit unions directly, because career changers are hired by people who have met them, not by filters.
High ghost risk. Two controls postings on my board have been open since February and both plants told me they are always accepting applications. Toledo.

Always accepting applications means a real shortage of controls people, not a fake role, and it is the best news on your board. Apply to both, then follow up with the maintenance manager rather than HR, because in a shortage the technical manager decides and the requisition follows. Two searches a day.
