Use the same logic across your market: plants near you are chronically short on PLC and robotics skills, so a direct approach to eight maintenance managers within commuting distance will produce more than any posting will. Track anything that comes back with Add role.
Nothing is moving. 16 cards in Sourced, one application in two months. Senior policy analyst, Sacramento, housing.

Policy hiring runs on cycles and networks, and a board that sits still for two months usually means you are waiting for perfect fits that will not arrive. Two searches a day and three applications this month. Sort the 16 by whether the organization works on housing production or on something adjacent; apply to the housing ones now and treat the rest as background. For research organizations, send the report you authored along with the application rather than only a resume, because in this field the work sample is what gets read. Close anything requiring a doctorate you do not have.
Too few results. Director of store operations, Atlanta. Titles 'VP retail operations'. Keywords 'multi-unit, labor model, shrink, four-wall margin, mystery shop'. Locations 'Atlanta'.

One executive title plus five retail-specific phrases plus one metro is why the board is empty. Add 'director of retail operations, director of field operations, VP stores' to titles so both your current level and the step up are in range, and cut keywords to 'multi-unit, labor model, shrink'. Add 'Charlotte, Nashville, Remote' to locations, since retail headquarters roles concentrate in a few cities and executives are recruited across regions. Two runs a day. Specialty retail leadership moves through recruiters, so make sure two of them know your fleet size and your margin results.

All C and D fits. Director of last-mile, Philadelphia. Titles 'operations director'. Keywords 'logistics, delivery, operations'. Seniority 'Director'. I get warehouse supervisor and courier driver roles.

'Delivery' and 'operations' together match everything from driver postings to facility supervision, which is what you are seeing. Replace keywords with 'last mile, cost per stop, route density, delivery service partner, parcel'. Change titles to 'director of last mile, VP delivery operations, director of delivery, head of last mile' and set seniority to Director and VP. That will cut the board hard and raise the fit scores of what survives. Two runs a day. Retailers building their own delivery networks and regional carriers list these roles internally long before a board sees them, so check those directly.
How do I know whether my keywords are working? Everything looks plausible but the fit scores are mediocre.

Test them against a role you know is right. Find one posting you would definitely take, add it with Add role, and get a match report on it. The words that report says are covered are the ones your keywords should contain; the words it flags as missing are either gaps to address or noise to ignore. Then check whether your current Settings keywords appear in that posting at all. If they do not, the scan is filtering on vocabulary the market does not use. Change them, run the search twice, and compare the fit scores on the new results before changing anything else.
I keep seeing the same handful of companies over and over. My board looks like four employers.

That usually means your keywords match those employers' house style rather than your profession, and the scan is honestly reporting a narrow filter. Loosen the vocabulary: replace any phrase that feels like internal jargon with the plainest industry-standard equivalent. Then deliberately diversify the channel rather than the query, because a single scan will always over-represent whoever posts most. Build a list of twenty employers you would work for, check their careers pages on a weekly rotation, and add openings with Add role. A board fed by two channels will show you a market; a board fed by one shows you a filter.
Should I be applying to roles that are a stretch, or only the ones I clearly qualify for?

Both, in a fixed ratio. Roughly a quarter of your applications should be a level or a domain above where you sit, because those are the roles that change your trajectory and occasionally land. The rest should be roles where you meet most of the stated requirements, because those are what pay the mortgage. The distinction that matters is which kind of gap: a missing system, tool or industry is learnable and worth stretching on, while a missing license, degree or hard year-count requirement will be screened out before a human reads it. Sort your board that way and the ratio takes care of itself.
My Sourced column has 40 cards and I feel behind every time I open the board. What should I actually do with them?

Empty it in one sitting. Give each card ninety seconds and one of three outcomes: research it this week, apply now, or Closed. Most will be Closed, and that is the point, because a Sourced column functions as a to-do list and forty items is not one. Then change the intake rate so it does not refill: two searches a day rather than ten, and no new cards added on a day when nothing moved right. A board of eight live roles you are actively working is worth more than forty you are avoiding, and it will read accurately when you open it.
I am considering a career change into a different industry. Should I change my search settings all at once?

No, run them side by side for a few weeks. Keep your current industry as the primary setting, because those roles pay and you may need one. Then once or twice a week, swap the titles and keywords to the target industry, run two searches, and add the results with Add role. What you are buying with that weekly pass is vocabulary: which titles the new industry uses, which of your skills appear in its postings, and which requirements repeat. Once you can predict what a posting will ask for, switch the primary setting over and rewrite the resume in that language.
The follow-ups strip keeps showing roles I applied to weeks ago and I do not know what to say. I have been ignoring it.

Ignoring it is why nothing has moved. A follow-up works when it adds something rather than asking for a status, so send two or three sentences that reference a specific detail from the posting and say one useful thing about how you would approach it. Send it to the hiring manager rather than the recruiting inbox where you can identify them. If a role is more than a month past the follow-up date with no reply after one attempt, move it to Closed; keeping it open distorts your sense of how many live conversations you actually have, which is the number that matters.
When should I stop adjusting my search and accept that the settings are as good as they will get?

When two runs a day produce three to eight roles you would genuinely apply to, and the fit scores cluster above the middle. At that point further tuning has diminishing returns and the constraint moves downstream to how many quality applications you send and how many conversations you start. The signal that you have over-tuned is a board that is small and comfortable, where everything scores well because you have filtered out anything that would challenge you. If you have gone three days without seeing a role that scares you slightly, widen one filter and see what comes back.
I set my seniority filter to exactly my current level and now I wonder if that was right. Should I be looking up, down, or both?

Set it to your level plus one above, and check the one below manually every few weeks rather than leaving it on. Looking up is where the growth roles are and titles inflate and deflate between companies, so a level above often describes the job you already do. Looking down is worth an occasional pass because a smaller company's version of a junior title sometimes carries more scope than your current seat. What you should not do is leave all three levels on permanently, because the volume buries the roles that matter and every fit score lands in the middle.
